dashboard.title: Teacher pages for Python Foundation
dashboard.subtitle: A complete set of teacher guides for online or offline instruction, aligned with the 24 learner lessons, quick quizzes, code exercises, test cases, and homework.
principles: Teach in a cycle: hook → short explanation → model → learner practice → immediate feedback.
Connect every concept to a real-life example before introducing Python syntax.
Do not fix the whole program for learners; ask questions so they compare Input, Expected output, and Actual output.
Provide support for struggling learners and challenges for fast learners so the class stays balanced.
End every lesson with a small product or clear evidence of learning.
dashboard.onlineOfflineDesc: Each guide includes adaptations for live online classes and in-person classroom teaching.
dashboard.pacingDesc: Teachers can follow the recommended duration or compress it depending on the group.
dashboard.teacherTip: For younger students, prioritize visuals and pair programming. For adults, use workplace contexts such as reports, tables, files, and automation.
dashboard.lessonMap: Teacher guides aligned to the learner roadmap
dashboard.lessonMapDesc: Each guide includes objectives, preparation, teaching script, questions, classwork, homework, quiz answer key, and assessment rubric.

pythonFoundation.title: Python Foundation - Teacher Guide
pythonFoundation.description: A roadmap for absolute beginners, grade 6-12 students, learners with weak foundations, and learners preparing for projects, automation, data, or AI.
learnerProfiles: Learners with no programming background or very limited exposure.
Younger students who need visual examples, small games, and short activities.
Adult learners who need practical contexts such as data, files, and repeated work.
Learners who must build debugging habits instead of copying code.

deliveryModes.title: Online
deliveryModes.description: Suitable for Zoom/Meet classes or 1-on-1 tutoring. Teachers use screen sharing, chat for quick answers, and CodeRunner for learner testing.
checklist: Open both the learner lesson and the teacher guide before class.
Ask learners to share screens or screenshots when tests fail.
Use breakout rooms for pair work in larger groups.

deliveryModes.title: Offline
deliveryModes.description: Suitable for center-based classes. Teachers can use a whiteboard, question cards, pair work, and walking checks at learner computers.
checklist: Prepare an IPO/flow board or syntax board.
Walk around during practice to catch indentation, quotation, and input mistakes.
Let learners present their output to the class.

assessmentPlan: Quick quizzes check concepts and common mistakes.
Code exercises use test cases to verify real output, especially when input() is involved.
Homework checks whether learners can work independently after class.
The final project is assessed by functionality, correctness, code organization, presentation, and debugging ability.
pacingNotes: Lessons 1-4 should move slowly so learners understand input/output and how the computer reads instructions.
Lessons 5-10 are where syntax and data-type mistakes are common; use many stdin test runs.
Lessons 11-18 focus on organizing code, functions, debugging, and decomposing problems.
Lessons 19-24 move toward products: files, data, mini projects, and final project.

teacher.title: Teacher guide: Can computers think?
teacher.summary: Teacher guide for “Can computers think?”, designed so learners understand, practice, and debug independently. Focus: Input - Process - Output and the idea that computers follow explicit instructions.
teacher.focus: Input - Process - Output and the idea that computers follow explicit instructions

materials: Teacher and learner computers; browser opened to the learning website.
Whiteboard or online board for Input - Process - Output or core syntax.
One correct example and one intentionally wrong example for debugging.
Quick question sheet or chat prompt for 30-second feedback.
checklist: Read the learner lesson and run the sample code before class.
Identify the two most common mistakes in the lesson.
Prepare one support question for struggling learners and one challenge for fast learners.
Check the code exercise/test cases if the lesson includes CodeRunner.
onlineSetup: Open the teacher guide, learner lesson, and code runner tabs.
Use chat to collect output predictions before running code.
When learners fail, ask for Input, Expected output, and Actual output.
offlineSetup: Write the lesson goal and core syntax on the board before class.
Prepare 2-3 question cards for random calling.
Walk around during practice to catch indentation, quotation, and data-type mistakes.

hook.title: Engaging opening
hook.story: Quick challenge: if you tell a robot “do my homework”, what exact steps would the robot still need?
prompts: Why is Input - Process - Output and the idea that computers follow explicit instructions important when learning programming?
If we did this by hand, how many steps would we need?
Before running the program, what output do you predict?
expectedResponses: Learners give a real-life or classroom example.
Learners begin using terms such as input, processing, output, condition, loop, or data.
Learners understand that predicting before running improves debugging.

flow.title: Opening and objectives
teacherActions: State the lesson goal and connect it to Input - Process - Output and the idea that computers follow explicit instructions.
Use the hook: Quick challenge: if you tell a robot “do my homework”, what exact steps would the robot still need?
Ask learners to predict the result before explaining syntax.
learnerActions: Answer quickly aloud or in chat.
Give a similar real-life example.
Write one thing they want to understand clearly.
flow.check: At least 70% of learners can say what problem today’s lesson helps solve.

flow.title: Core explanation
teacherActions: Draw or present the model: IPO model: input data → processing steps → output result.
Explain each idea in short sentences and avoid unnecessary jargon.
Connect the new concept to the previous lesson.
learnerActions: Write down key terms.
Answer check-for-understanding questions after each point.
Compare the new example with a previous one.
flow.check: Learners can explain the concept in their own words.

flow.title: Think-aloud modeling
teacherActions: Model step by step with this activity: Learners analyze a daily process such as making noodles, logging in, or calculating a score as Input, Process, and Output.
Think aloud about why each line or step is needed.
Ask learners to predict output before running code.
learnerActions: Predict the output.
Identify which step produces the output.
Ask questions when output differs from their prediction.
flow.check: Learners predict at least one sample output correctly or explain why their prediction was wrong.

flow.title: Guided practice
teacherActions: Run the activity: Each pair chooses one daily activity, writes three IPO lines, and presents it in 45 seconds.
Walk around or check screens to catch small mistakes early.
Guide with questions rather than fixing the whole task.
learnerActions: Try individually first, then ask a partner.
Run code or complete the activity sheet.
Compare their result with the success criteria.
flow.check: Each learner/pair has a working result or a clear written analysis.

flow.title: Quick quiz/check
teacherActions: Let learners complete the web quiz or answer 3 oral checkpoint questions.
Explain the 1-2 questions most learners miss.
If there is a coding task, require learners to run tests before declaring it correct.
learnerActions: Complete the quiz individually.
Compare answers with explanations.
Write one mistake to avoid.
flow.check: Target score is 70% or higher, or learners correct the main misconception.

flow.title: Wrap-up and assignment
teacherActions: Summarize: today learners practiced Input - Process - Output and the idea that computers follow explicit instructions.
Assign homework and make submission format explicit.
Preview the next lesson so learners know what to prepare.
learnerActions: Submit an exit ticket or answer the final question.
Record homework.
Self-rate their understanding.
flow.check: Learners know what to do after class and how to check their work.

teachingScript.title: Set the context
teachingScript.say: Today we do not memorize syntax first. We start from the problem: Input - Process - Output and the idea that computers follow explicit instructions. Once the problem is clear, the Python syntax has a reason to exist.
teachingScript.demo: IPO model: input data → processing steps → output result
teachingScript.ask: If we remove one step from this model, can the result still be correct?

teachingScript.title: Explain with a small example
teachingScript.say: I will model slowly. Your job is to predict first, then check whether the computer behaves as you expected.
teachingScript.demo: Learners analyze a daily process such as making noodles, logging in, or calculating a score as Input, Process, and Output.
teachingScript.ask: What data goes in? Which line processes it? Which line creates the output?

teachingScript.title: Move to practice
teachingScript.say: Now you try. If it fails, do not delete everything. Read the error or compare the actual output with the expected output.
teachingScript.demo: Fix one small issue, run again, and record what changed.
teachingScript.ask: Is your issue in the input, processing, or output?

liveCoding.title: Live coding with the same learner workflow
steps: Open the same coding exercise that learners use, not a separate environment.
If the task uses input(), type sample data in Standard input and run manually first.
Then run code tests and explain each test case: input, expected output, actual output.
When a test fails, fix one small issue, run again, and ask learners why it now passes.
mistakesToTrigger: Saying “the computer understands by itself” instead of needing clear instructions.
Confusing output with a processing action in the middle.
debrief: Identify whether the issue was in input, processing, or output.
Remind learners to predict output before running.
Write one debugging rule on the board.

activities.title: Pair activity: predict before running
instructions: Each pair chooses one daily activity, writes three IPO lines, and presents it in 45 seconds.
Each pair must state the input, processing step, and predicted output.
After running/discussing, write what differed between prediction and real result.
activities.successCriteria: The pair can explain the result verbally, not just say “right/wrong”.

activities.title: Individual guided practice
instructions: Learners analyze a daily process such as making noodles, logging in, or calculating a score as Input, Process, and Output.
Learners work independently for 5-7 minutes before asking.
Teacher guides with questions and lets learners make the fix.
activities.successCriteria: Learner completes the task or can clearly identify the current bug and next fix.

warmup: What problem does Input - Process - Output and the idea that computers follow explicit instructions solve today?
Before writing code, how would you describe the task step by step?
What are the input and output of the opening example?
during: Which line or step shows this model: IPO model: input data → processing steps → output result?
If the sample input changes, how should the output change?
What mistake is most likely at this step?
exitTicket: Explain today’s concept in your own words.
Name one mistake you made or could make in this lesson.
What must be submitted for homework?

required: Write 3 everyday processes using IPO. Each process must have at least 2 inputs and 1 output.
Submit or screenshot the correct output together with your code.
Write 3-5 reflection lines: what you understood, what is still confusing, and what you debugged.
optional: Describe IPO for an online learning app: login, study a lesson, and view a score.
Create one extra input set and write the expected output before running.
homework.submissionFormat: Submit a .py file or code text, plus screenshot/output and a short reflection.

evidence: Learner completes the class activity or explains the approach correctly.
Learner scores at least 70% on the quick quiz or corrects missed questions after feedback.
If there is a coding task, learner passes test cases and explains why the output is correct.
Homework shows clear code/thinking rather than only copied examples.

rubric.excellent: Explains with an original example and uses key terms correctly.
rubric.good: States the main idea and recognizes correct examples.
rubric.needsWork: Still memorizes wording without distinguishing core parts.

rubric.excellent: Works independently, runs correctly, and fixes small bugs.
rubric.good: Completes the task with some hints.
rubric.needsWork: Needs step-by-step teacher guidance.

rubric.excellent: Compares input/expected/actual and fixes systematically.
rubric.good: Reads basic errors but still relies on trial and error.
rubric.needsWork: Deletes code or guesses when errors appear.

rubric.excellent: Explains the product and code decisions clearly.
rubric.good: Presents the main result.
rubric.needsWork: Can run code but cannot explain why it works.

commonMisconceptions: Saying “the computer understands by itself” instead of needing clear instructions.
Confusing output with a processing action in the middle.
Writing a process too generally without clear steps.
differentiation: Struggling learners: use fill-in templates, fewer input cases, and smaller step-by-step questions.
Fast learners: ask them to create extra test cases or improve output.
Online class: collect predictions in chat, then invite 1-2 learners to share screen for debugging.
Offline class: draw the data flow on the board, then let learners peer-review at another computer.

solution.notes: Reference solution for teacher checking only. Do not reveal it before learners attempt, run, and test their own code.

Explain each idea in short sentences and avoid unnecessary jargon.
Connect the new concept to the previous lesson.
learnerActions: Write down key terms.
Answer check-for-understanding questions after each point.
Compare the new example with a previous one.
flow.check: Learners can explain the concept in their own words.

flow.title: Think-aloud modeling
teacherActions: Model step by step with this activity: Learners run with scores 6 and 4.5 to observe different branches.
Think aloud about why each line or step is needed.
Ask learners to predict output before running code.
learnerActions: Predict the output.
Identify which step produces the output.
Ask questions when output differs from their prediction.
flow.check: Learners predict at least one sample output correctly or explain why their prediction was wrong.

flow.title: Guided practice
teacherActions: Run the activity: One learner reads a rule, the other converts the rule into a Python condition.
Walk around or check screens to catch small mistakes early.
Guide with questions rather than fixing the whole task.
learnerActions: Try individually first, then ask a partner.
Run code or complete the activity sheet.
Compare their result with the success criteria.
flow.check: Each learner/pair has a working result or a clear written analysis.

flow.title: Quick quiz/check
teacherActions: Let learners complete the web quiz or answer 3 oral checkpoint questions.
Explain the 1-2 questions most learners miss.
If there is a coding task, require learners to run tests before declaring it correct.
learnerActions: Complete the quiz individually.
Compare answers with explanations.
Write one mistake to avoid.
flow.check: Target score is 70% or higher, or learners correct the main misconception.

flow.title: Wrap-up and assignment
teacherActions: Summarize: today learners practiced if, true/false conditions, and decisions.
Assign homework and make submission format explicit.
Preview the next lesson so learners know what to prepare.
learnerActions: Submit an exit ticket or answer the final question.
Record homework.
Self-rate their understanding.
flow.check: Learners know what to do after class and how to check their work.

teachingScript.title: Set the context
teachingScript.say: Today we do not memorize syntax first. We start from the problem: if, true/false conditions, and decisions. Once the problem is clear, the Python syntax has a reason to exist.
teachingScript.demo: if checks a condition; when it is true, the indented block runs
teachingScript.ask: If we remove one step from this model, can the result still be correct?

teachingScript.title: Explain with a small example
teachingScript.say: I will model slowly. Your job is to predict first, then check whether the computer behaves as you expected.
teachingScript.demo: Learners run with scores 6 and 4.5 to observe different branches.
teachingScript.ask: What data goes in? Which line processes it? Which line creates the output?

teachingScript.title: Move to practice
teachingScript.say: Now you try. If it fails, do not delete everything. Read the error or compare the actual output with the expected output.
teachingScript.demo: Fix one small issue, run again, and record what changed.
teachingScript.ask: Is your issue in the input, processing, or output?

liveCoding.title: Live coding with the same learner workflow
steps: Open the same coding exercise that learners use, not a separate environment.
If the task uses input(), type sample data in Standard input and run manually first.
Then run code tests and explain each test case: input, expected output, actual output.
When a test fails, fix one small issue, run again, and ask learners why it now passes.
mistakesToTrigger: Forgetting the colon after if.
Using wrong indentation so a line is not inside the if block.
debrief: Identify whether the issue was in input, processing, or output.
Remind learners to predict output before running.
Write one debugging rule on the board.

activities.title: Pair activity: predict before running
instructions: One learner reads a rule, the other converts the rule into a Python condition.
Each pair must state the input, processing step, and predicted output.
After running/discussing, write what differed between prediction and real result.
activities.successCriteria: The pair can explain the result verbally, not just say “right/wrong”.

activities.title: Individual guided practice
instructions: Learners run with scores 6 and 4.5 to observe different branches.
Learners work independently for 5-7 minutes before asking.
Teacher guides with questions and lets learners make the fix.
activities.successCriteria: Learner completes the task or can clearly identify the current bug and next fix.

warmup: What problem does if, true/false conditions, and decisions solve today?
Before writing code, how would you describe the task step by step?
What are the input and output of the opening example?
during: Which line or step shows this model: if checks a condition; when it is true, the indented block runs?
If the sample input changes, how should the output change?
What mistake is most likely at this step?
exitTicket: Explain today’s concept in your own words.
Name one mistake you made or could make in this lesson.
What must be submitted for homework?

required: Write a program that inputs age; if age is at least 13 print “Teen learner”, otherwise print “Junior learner”.
Submit or screenshot the correct output together with your code.
Write 3-5 reflection lines: what you understood, what is still confusing, and what you debugged.
optional: Add validation for scores from 0 to 10.
Create one extra input set and write the expected output before running.

evidence: Learner completes the class activity or explains the approach correctly.
Learner scores at least 70% on the quick quiz or corrects missed questions after feedback.
If there is a coding task, learner passes test cases and explains why the output is correct.
Homework shows clear code/thinking rather than only copied examples.

rubric.good: States the main idea and recognizes correct examples.
rubric.needsWork: Still memorizes wording without distinguishing core parts.

rubric.good: Completes the task with some hints.
rubric.needsWork: Needs step-by-step teacher guidance.

rubric.excellent: Compares input/expected/actual and fixes systematically.

teacher.title: Teacher guide: Final project and review
teacher.summary: Teacher guide for “Final project and review”, designed so learners understand, practice, and debug independently. Focus: Final Project: requirements, coding, testing, improvement, and presentation.
teacher.focus: Final Project: requirements, coding, testing, improvement, and presentation

materials: Teacher and learner computers; browser opened to the learning website.
Whiteboard or online board for Input - Process - Output or core syntax.
One correct example and one intentionally wrong example for debugging.
Quick question sheet or chat prompt for 30-second feedback.
checklist: Read the learner lesson and run the sample code before class.
Identify the two most common mistakes in the lesson.
Prepare one support question for struggling learners and one challenge for fast learners.
Check the code exercise/test cases if the lesson includes CodeRunner.
onlineSetup: Open the teacher guide, learner lesson, and code runner tabs.
Use chat to collect output predictions before running code.
When learners fail, ask for Input, Expected output, and Actual output.
offlineSetup: Write the lesson goal and core syntax on the board before class.
Prepare 2-3 question cards for random calling.
Walk around during practice to catch indentation, quotation, and data-type mistakes.

hook.title: Engaging opening
hook.story: Ask whether a good final product only needs to run, or also needs to be understandable and presentable.
prompts: Why is Final Project: requirements, coding, testing, improvement, and presentation important when learning programming?
If we did this by hand, how many steps would we need?
Before running the program, what output do you predict?
expectedResponses: Learners give a real-life or classroom example.
Learners begin using terms such as input, processing, output, condition, loop, or data.
Learners understand that predicting before running improves debugging.

flow.title: Opening and objectives
teacherActions: State the lesson goal and connect it to Final Project: requirements, coding, testing, improvement, and presentation.
Use the hook: Ask whether a good final product only needs to run, or also needs to be understandable and presentable.
Ask learners to predict the result before explaining syntax.
learnerActions: Answer quickly aloud or in chat.
Give a similar real-life example.
Write one thing they want to understand clearly.
flow.check: At least 70% of learners can say what problem today’s lesson helps solve.

flow.title: Core explanation
teacherActions: Draw or present the model: A final project needs clear requirements, feature breakdown, tests, improvement, and presentation.
Explain each idea in short sentences and avoid unnecessary jargon.
Connect the new concept to the previous lesson.
learnerActions: Write down key terms.
Answer check-for-understanding questions after each point.
Compare the new example with a previous one.
flow.check: Learners can explain the concept in their own words.

flow.title: Think-aloud modeling
teacherActions: Model step by step with this activity: Learners choose a project topic, write a feature backlog, and define done criteria.
Think aloud about why each line or step is needed.
Ask learners to predict output before running code.
learnerActions: Predict the output.
Identify which step produces the output.
Ask questions when output differs from their prediction.
flow.check: Learners predict at least one sample output correctly or explain why their prediction was wrong.

flow.title: Guided practice
teacherActions: Run the activity: Each pair reviews another learner’s project plan using a checklist.
Walk around or check screens to catch small mistakes early.
Guide with questions rather than fixing the whole task.
learnerActions: Try individually first, then ask a partner.
Run code or complete the activity sheet.
Compare their result with the success criteria.
flow.check: Each learner/pair has a working result or a clear written analysis.

flow.title: Quick quiz/check
teacherActions: Let learners complete the web quiz or answer 3 oral checkpoint questions.
Explain the 1-2 questions most learners miss.
If there is a coding task, require learners to run tests before declaring it correct.
learnerActions: Complete the quiz individually.
Compare answers with explanations.
Write one mistake to avoid.
flow.check: Target score is 70% or higher, or learners correct the main misconception.

flow.title: Wrap-up and assignment
teacherActions: Summarize: today learners practiced Final Project: requirements, coding, testing, improvement, and presentation.
Assign homework and make submission format explicit.
Preview the next lesson so learners know what to prepare.
learnerActions: Submit an exit ticket or answer the final question.
Record homework.
Self-rate their understanding.
flow.check: Learners know what to do after class and how to check their work.

teachingScript.demo: Learners choose a project topic, write a feature backlog, and define done criteria.
teachingScript.ask: What data goes in? Which line processes it? Which line creates the output?

teachingScript.ask: Is your issue in the input, processing, or output?
